title: Genetic testing in cardiomyopathies: do we need to redefine the UK national testing criteria?
abstract:
Introduction: Inherited cardiac conditions, including dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), may have a monogenic cause identified through genetic testing (GT). Confirmation of pathogenic gene variants can have important implications for the patient and their relatives. The UK National Genomic Test Directory (NGTD) provides strict criteria on the indications for GT; however, the European Society of Cardiology (ESC) recommends wider GT. We reviewed the prevalence of pathogenic genotypes in patients undergoing GT who did not meet the NGTD criteria.

Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of patients who underwent GT with a confirmed diagnosis of HCM or DCM attending the Essex Inherited Cardiac Conditions Clinic between January 2023 and January 2025. Results 257 patients were included in the analysis, with 136 patients with DCM (52.9%) and 121 patients with HCM (47.1%). The diagnostic yield of GT was 19.9% in DCM and 17.4% in HCM. 14.8% of gene-positive patients with DCM and 14.3% of gene-positive patients with HCM did not meet current UK testing criteria, predominantly due to age of onset. All gene-positive patients in the DCM subgroup not meeting current NGTD criteria for testing had evidence of myocardial fibrosis.

Conclusion: A significant minority of patients (1 in 7) with cardiomyopathy and a pathogenic genotype did not meet current UK testing criteria; each patient has an average of 4 first-degree relatives at risk who will benefit from predictive GT. We propose the adoption of the wider ESC guidance, removing the strict age-related cut-offs and being guided more by the severity of the phenotype, particularly involving myocardial scarring.

Genetic testing in cardiomyopathies: do 
we need to redefine the UK national 
testing criteria?
Andrew Chisom Madu,1 Anthony David Dimarco,1,2 Amy Hardy-­Wallace,1 
Archana Ganapathy,1,2 Henry Oluwasefunmi Savage  ‍ ‍ ,1,2 Jason N Dungu  ‍ ‍ 1,2
Viewpoint

employer(s)) 2026. Re-­use

and permissions. Published by 
BMJ Group.
ABSTRACT
Introduction  Inherited cardiac conditions, including 
dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) and hypertrophic 
cardiomyopathy (HCM), may have a monogenic cause 
identified through genetic testing (GT). Confirmation of 
pathogenic gene variants can have important implications 
for the patient and their relatives. The UK National 
Genomic Test Directory (NGTD) provides strict criteria on 
the indications for GT; however, the European Society of 
Cardiology (ESC) recommends wider GT. We reviewed 
the prevalence of pathogenic genotypes in patients 
undergoing GT who did not meet the NGTD criteria.
Methods  We conducted a retrospective analysis of 
patients who underwent GT with a confirmed diagnosis 
of HCM or DCM attending the Essex Inherited Cardiac 
Conditions Clinic between January 2023 and January 
2025.
Results  257 patients were included in the analysis, with 
136 patients with DCM (52.9%) and 121 patients with 
HCM (47.1%). The diagnostic yield of GT was 19.9% in 
DCM and 17.4% in HCM.
14.8% of gene-­positive patients with DCM and 14.3% 
of gene-­positive patients with HCM did not meet current 
UK testing criteria, predominantly due to age of onset. 
All gene-­positive patients in the DCM subgroup not 
meeting current NGTD criteria for testing had evidence of 
myocardial fibrosis.
Conclusion  A significant minority of patients (1 in 7) 
with cardiomyopathy and a pathogenic genotype did 
not meet current UK testing criteria; each patient has an 
average of 4 first-­degree relatives at risk who will benefit 
from predictive GT. We propose the adoption of the wider 
ESC guidance, removing the strict age-­related cut-­offs 
and being guided more by the severity of the phenotype, 
particularly involving myocardial scarring.
INTRODUCTION
Among the spectrum of Inherited Cardiac 
Conditions (ICC), the myocardial disorders 
dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) and hyper-
trophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) represent 
the most prevalent subtypes encountered in 
clinical practice. Although the majority of 
cardiomyopathy phenotypes are not due to 
monogenic causes, identification of a path-
ogenic or likely pathogenic gene variant has 
important implications for first-­degree rela-
tives and may guide therapies in the proband 
(index case) as well as cascade screening with 
predictive genetic testing (GT).1 2
UK National Genomic Test Directory (NGTD)
The UK NGTD established specific criteria 
to guide the referral and triage of patients 
for genomic testing across a wide range of 
inherited diseases.3 The NGTD is commis-
sioned by the National Health Service (NHS) 
in England, and its focus is to guide requests 
that will have high yield for results that are 
highly likely to change clinical management 
of the patient or their family. While cost impli-
cations are not explicitly mentioned in the 
guidance, there is clearly a need to rationalise 
resources within a public healthcare system. 
However, strict adherence to such guidelines 
may exclude patients who have pathogenic 
variants but fall outside the defined criteria. 
The DCM/ACM (arrhythmogenic cardio-
myopathy) R132 subpanel and HCM R131 
subpanel genes and testing criteria are listed 
in figure 1.
Why may patients be tested outside of the criteria 
for DCM?
Prior to the first European Society of Cardi-
ology (ESC) cardiomyopathy guidelines 
published in 2023, there was an ill-­defined 
cardiomyopathy phenotype, now termed 
non-­dilated left ventricular cardiomyopathy 
(NDLVC), which is consistent with a non-­
ischaemic cardiomyopathy complicated by 
scarring but without objective left ventricular 
(LV) dilatation.4 The guidelines clearly 
described the high prevalence of scar burden 
in NDLVC and its correlation with an under-
lying genetic substrate.
The absence of a family history does not 
exclude the possibility of a familial condition, 
with phenotypic heterogeneity common in 
some variants. There is also a possibility of 
familial deaths being deemed non-­cardiac or 
Open Heart: first published as 10.1136/openhrt-2025-003838 on 21 January 2026. Downloaded from https://openheart.bmj.com on 6 February 2026 by guest.
Protected by copyright, including for uses related to text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
Open Heart
2
Madu AC, et al. Open Heart 2026;13:e003838. doi:10.1136/openhrt-2025-003838
not sudden cardiac death related in error, due to incom-
plete investigation or in the absence of postmortem 
examination of deceased relatives. The clinical suspicion 
of an unproven but suspected familial condition some-
times prompts genotyping outside of the defined criteria.
Why may patients be tested outside of the criteria for HCM?
Patients may have a clear HCM phenotype but not meet 
strict criteria based on absolute wall thickness. There is 
increasing evidence that an absolute cut-­off of 15 mm 
wall thickness is flawed, as it fails to account for body size 
indexing, or the natural apical tapering of the myocar-
dium; thus, studies have proposed an adjustment of this 
criterion.5 Patients presenting with an early phenotype 
detected surreptitiously, for example, through an ECG 
performed routinely for non-­cardiology reasons such 
as preoperative screening, may be declined genotyping 
due to wall thickness, despite very high clinical suspicion. 
Missed early diagnoses may have important implications 
for the wider family, with up to 60% penetrance in some 
sarcomeric gene variants.6
This study describes GT in a cohort of patients with 
DCM and HCM in the ICC service at the tertiary Essex 
Cardiothoracic Centre. The NGTD has a much wider 
scope, but we have focused on the most prevalent cardiac 
Figure 1  Gene panels and testing criteria for cardiomyopathies according to the UK NGTD. (A) Gene panel (R132)11 and the 
testing criteria in dilated and arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy.9 (B) Gene panel (R131)12 and the testing criteria in hypertrophic 
cardiomyopathy.10 Titin (TTN), lamin A/C (LMNA), filamin C (FLNC), phospholamban (PLN), desmin (DES), desmoplakin (DSP), 
RNA-­binding protein 20 (RBM20), sodium channel type V (SCN5A), actin alpha cardiac muscle 1 (ACTC1), actinin alpha 2 
(ACTN2), Bcl2-­associated athanogene 3 (BAG3), cadherin 2 (CDH2), dystrophin (DMD), dolichol kinase (DOLK), desmocollin 2 
(DSC2), desmoglein 2 (DSG2), emerin (EMD), junction plakoglobin (JUP), lysosomal associated membrane protein 2 (LAMP2), 
myosin-­binding protein C3 (MYBPC3), myosin heavy chain 7 (MYH7), nexilin F-­actin binding protein (NEXN), NK2 homeobox 
5 (NKX2-­5), plakophilin 2 (PKP2), transmembrane protein 43 (TMEM43), troponin C1 slow skeletal and cardiac type (TNNC1), 
troponin I3 cardiac type (TNNI3), troponin I interacting kinase (TNNI3K), troponin T2 cardiac type (TNNT2), tropomyosin 1 
(alpha) (TPM1), vinculin (VCL), domains 1 (FHL1), formin homology 2 domain containing 3 (FHOD3), junctophilin 2 (JPH2), 
myosin light chain 2 (MYL2), myosin light chain 3 (MYL3), protein kinase AMP-­activated non-­catalytic subunit gamma 2 
(PRKAG2), calcium voltage-­gated channel subunit alpha1 C, p.Arg518 variant (CACNA1C, p.Arg518), mitochondrially encoded 
tRNA isoleucine m.4300A>G variant (MT-­TI, m.4300A>G), transthyretin (TTR), galactosidase alpha (GLA). AMP, adenosine 
monophosphate, NGTD, National Genomic Test Directory.

Protected by copyright, including for uses related to text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
3
Madu AC, et al. Open Heart 2026;13:e003838. doi:10.1136/openhrt-2025-003838
Viewpoint
conditions, DCM and HCM, for this analysis. The primary 
aim was to evaluate the diagnostic yield of testing, and the 
prevalence of genetic variants being identified in patients 
whose clinical presentations and background prompted 
testing but were not fully captured within the NGTD 
criteria.
METHOD
We analysed available results for all patients who under-
went GT in the Essex ICC clinic between January 2023 
and January 2025. Individuals who underwent predictive 
testing or clinical screening alone were excluded. All 
patients with DCM and HCM were categorised into two 
groups: fully met the criteria and did not fully meet the 
criteria.
Patient and public involvement
Patients/public were first involved in this research 
through discussion in the Essex ICC Clinic Patient and 
Family Support Group; approval of the study objectives 
was confirmed, and dissemination of the peer-­reviewed 
findings will be via this forum.
Statistical analysis
Descriptive statistics were used to summarise patient 
demographics and baseline characteristics. All statistical 
analysis was performed using Microsoft Excel. Contin-
uous variables were reported as means±SD or medians 
with IQR, and categorical variables were expressed as 
counts/frequencies and percentages. Continuous data 
were compared using a two-­sample t-­test, and categorical 
data were compared using the χ2 test. Gene-­positive is 
defined as the detection of a pathogenic or likely path-
ogenic variant; therefore, variants of uncertain signifi-
cance were excluded for the purpose of analysis.
RESULTS
A total of 257 patients with DCM and HCM had results 
available, totalling 136 patients with DCM (52.9%) and 
121 patients with HCM (47.1%) (table 1). Out of the total 
cohort, the diagnostic yield from GT was 18.7%.
DCM subgroup
Of the 136 DCM patients, 84 (61.8%) were men and 52 
(38.2%) were women. The median age of this cohort 
was 56 (IQR 43.5–64.0) years. The median age at diag-
nosis was 52 (IQR 39.8–60.0) years. 103 (75.7%) patients 
met the NGTD testing criteria, while the remaining 33 
(24.3%) did not. In total, 27 patients with DCM (19.9%) 
were gene-­positive (ie, the diagnostic yield), and the 
most common gene found was titin (TTN) (n=16, 59.3% 
of gene-­positive results in DCM). Other genes identified 
included lamin A/C (LMNA) (n=3), myosin heavy chain 
7 (MYH7) (n=3), actin alpha cardiac muscle 1 (ACTC1) 
(n=2), filamin-­C protein (FLNC) (n=1), myosin-­binding 
protein C3 (MYBPC3) (n=1), sodium channel type V 
(SCN5A) (n=1) and desmoglein 2 (DSG2) (n=1). Four 
patients (14.8%) who were gene-­positive did not meet 
current testing criteria (summarised in figure 2A).
HCM subgroup
Of the 121 HCM patients, 88 (72.7%) were men, and 33 
(27.3%) were women. The median age of this cohort was 
60 (IQR 52.0–69.0) years. The median age at diagnosis was 
54 (IQR 46.0–65.0) years. The median age of symptom 
onset was 53 (IQR 45.0–63.0) years; higher in compar-
ison to the DCM group (p<0.05). The mean LV wall thick-
ness was 22±4.2 mm. 74 patients with HCM (61.2%) met 
the current NGTD testing criteria, while the remaining 
47 (38.8%) did not. In total, 21 (17.4%) patients with 
HCM were gene-­positive, with the most common genes 
found being MYBPC3 (n=14, 66.7% of the gene-­positive 
results in HCM) and MYH7 (n=3, 14.3%). Other genes 
found include troponin I3 cardiac Type (TNNI3) (n=2), 
TTN (n=1), alpha-­protein kinase 3 (ALPK3) (n=1) and 
potassium voltage-­gated channel subfamily E regulatory 
subunit 1 (KCNE1) (n=1). Three gene-­positive patients 
with HCM (14.3%) did not meet the current testing UK 
NGTD criteria (summarised in figure 2B).
Screening of the seven gene-­positive, yet NGTD criteria 
negative, DCM/HCM patients has identified 28 first-­
degree relatives at risk and appropriate for predictive GT 
(median four relatives per proband, range 2–8).
DISCUSSION
While the majority of DCM and HCM patients met the 
UK NGTD criteria for GT, a notable proportion (1 in 7) 
of the gene-­positive results arose from testing outside the 
current iteration (14.8% in DCM versus 14.3% in HCM; 
p=0.96).
Proposal
The clinical implications of a positive genetic diagnosis 
cannot be understated. An abnormal genetic result does 
not always affect management of the patient, but it may 
inform risk stratification, with early consideration for 
intervention if required (eg, primary prevention implant-
able cardioverter defibrillator in LMNA or FLNC vari-
ants). A genetic diagnosis will also trigger further cascade 
screening, with predictive GT when appropriate and 
surveillance of high-­risk relatives.
The ESC have reported its criteria for the testing of 
patients who have cardiomyopathy.4 Important recom-
mendations for testing include:
	
►Those who fulfil phenotypical criteria of cardiomyo-
pathy, where it benefits their clinical management or 
enables cascade screening.
	
►Those who fulfil phenotypical criteria of cardio-
myopathy, where it benefits the patient struggling 
to understand their disease psychologically (even 
if it has no benefit in clinical management or for 
cascade screening), also taking into account patient 
preference.

►Those with a borderline phenotype, not enough to 
fulfil phenotypical criteria, after detailed assessment 
by specialist teams.
Although the ESC sets a broader framework for testing 
than the UK NGTD, most of the recommendations are 
based on level B or C evidence (derived from a single

genotype-­positive patients (n=48). P values reflect the comparison between the HCM and DCM groups.

5
Madu AC, et al. Open Heart 2026;13:e003838. doi:10.1136/openhrt-2025-003838
Viewpoint
randomised/large non-­randomised study or formed 
from a consensus of expert opinion, respectively); this 
highlights the paucity of data to guide testing algorithms. 
However, we hope the data reported in this study will 
prompt further discussion and review, with adaptation of 
the guidelines and directories encouraged.
Our opinion is that an age cut-­off in the absence of 
family history is punitive, given that 3 out of the 4 (75.0%) 
gene-­positive but NGTD-­criteria-­negative patients with 
DCM had an age of onset above 65 years (median age 
was 56 (IQR 43.5–64.0)). A similar pattern was seen in 
the HCM cohort, with 2 out of 3 gene-­positive but NGTD-­
criteria-­negative patients (66.6%) presenting with an age 
of onset above 60 years, strengthening our argument 
against the current age-­specific criteria.
While the NGTD remains a valuable tool for national 
standardisation, its future use may benefit from incor-
porating broader clinical discretion focusing on 
phenotypic-­driven criteria. There are potential cost 
implications to wider testing,7 8 but our data support a 
role for redefined testing criteria and consideration of 
bespoke subpanels with higher yield and potentially 
better cost-­effectiveness. The newly defined NDLVC with 
proven myocardial scarring warrants consideration of a 
dedicated yet smaller subpanel including LMNA, FLNC, 
phospholamban (PLN) and desmin (DES) gene variants, 
potentially without the need to include all variants associ-
ated with LV dilatation; further discussion between clini-
cians and genomic laboratories is encouraged to define 
the variants to be included. Similarly, consideration of a 
myocarditis subpanel including desmoplakin (DSP) and 
DSG2 gene variants is recommended, given the potential 
for ACM to mimic myocarditis in the ‘hot phase’.9 There 
is a tendency to attribute myocarditis to viral infections; 
however, the recent 2025 ESC guidelines recommend GT 
in patients with recurrent myocarditis, phenotypically 
mild cardiomyopathies in the context of a family history 
of myocarditis, or myocarditis presenting with severe 
ventricular arrhythmias.10
The authors acknowledge that the burden on the UK 
genomics laboratories is not purely financial, that is, the 
cost of testing. An increase in demand has resulted in the 
development of a significant backlog, affecting the turn-
around time for processing and results reporting. Any 
further increase in demand due to wider testing criteria 
will affect the timeliness of reporting, and this could 
have clinical implications. There is a finite capacity in 
the UK NGTD-­affiliated laboratories, however we hope 
the suggested amendments will streamline pathways, 
allowing more targeted testing and achievement of the 
42-­day target. We also accept that the genomics labora-
tories are always willing to discuss individual cases and 
proceed with testing in cases outside the criteria that have 
been discussed in a wider multidisciplinary team setting; 
however, requests sent routinely, without discussion, may 
be rejected based on the UK NGTD alone.
Gene elusive patients may benefit from future wider 
GT, either via retesting with panels including novel vari-
ants or as genome-­wide association studies. However, 
genotyping all patients attending ICC clinics without 
some inclusion and exclusion criteria is clearly inappro-
priate, given the cost implications, effect on turnaround 
time of results reporting, and significant risk of identi-
fying variants of uncertain significance with no clinical 
Figure 2  Overview of the gene-­positive patients who did not meet the UK NGTD criteria for genetic testing. (A) DCM patients 
with pathogenic variants not fulfilling NGTD criteria. (B) HCM patients with pathogenic variants not fulfilling NGTD criteria. 
Filamin-­C protein (FLNC), myosin-­binding protein C3 (MYBPC3), actin alpha cardiac muscle 1 (ACTC1), titin (TTN), alpha-­
protein kinase 3 (ALPK3), myosin heavy chain 7 (MYH7). NGTD, National Genomic Test Directory.
Open Heart: first published as 10.1136/openhrt-2025-003838 on 21 January 2026. Downloaded from https://openheart.bmj.com on 6 February 2026 by guest.

Open Heart
6
Madu AC, et al. Open Heart 2026;13:e003838. doi:10.1136/openhrt-2025-003838
relevance and causing unnecessary distress to patients 
and their relatives.
There is a process of predictive testing in families with 
pathogenic or likely pathogenic gene variants to detect 
relatives at risk. The absolute number of gene-­positive 
patients not meeting the current NGTD criteria in this 
study was low; however, this resulted in the identification 
of 28 individuals, an average of 4 first-­degree relatives per 
proband, appropriate for predictive GT. While a posi-
tive predictive genetic test enables early introduction of 
prognostic therapies to reduce the risk of future adverse 
events, it is equally beneficial to the patient and cost-­
saving for the NHS when first-­degree relatives are subse-
quently found to be gene negative, removing the need 
for life-­long clinical screening.
Limitations
This is a study of patients who were referred for GT mainly 
based on clinical suspicion of inherited disease; we acknowl-
edge that the retrospective analyses and reinterpretation 
of testing criteria are likely to include significant selection 
bias. However, within these limitations, the need to re-­eval-
uate the current testing criteria is justified by the results 
reported. We have not reported data regarding patients 
deemed inappropriate for genotyping, and we accept this 
group may also include gene-­positive patients with implica-
tions for wider screening.
The yield of GT overall was lower than might be expected 
in a tertiary referral centre. The potential reason for the 
low yield is referral from a relatively new ICC service, 
established for only 2 years at the time of data collection, 
which is continually defining and evolving its local criteria 
for requesting genetic tests. The analysis of the genotyped 
patients presented in this work is likely to improve the 
gene-­positive rates.
CONCLUSION
This analysis underscores the important role of GT in the 
diagnosis and management of cardiomyopathies. While 
the NGTD provides a structured and detailed framework 
to triage patients needing further testing, our data suggest 
the current criteria are restrictive, with risk of missing 
genotype-­positive patients. Rigid adherence to UK NGTD 
criteria may prevent early diagnosis in relatives at risk, thus 
limiting the potential for prompt initiation of prognostic 
therapies or promoting unnecessary long-­term clinical 
screening in individuals who would have benefited from a 
negative predictive test.
Given the increasing accessibility and affordability of 
GT, we believe re-­evaluation of the UK NGTD criteria is 
indicated and should allow for greater clinical discretion 
or a broader criteria description. Future studies involving 
larger cohorts and analyses are warranted to guide areas of 
refinement in the testing framework. The role of smaller 
subpanels in specific subtypes of cardiomyopathy, including 
NDLVC and some myocarditis cases, is evidence-­based and 
likely to be beneficial and potentially more cost-­effective.

Contributors  ACM and JND were responsible for study conception. AHW was 
responsible for data provision. AG assisted with manuscript review. ACM carried 
out data collection, review of patient records, statistical analysis and data 
interpretation. ACM drafted the manuscript and conducted the research related 
to the discussion. ADD and HOS provided senior clinical oversight, reviewed and 
edited the manuscript. JND provided overall supervision, helped interpret findings 
and was primarily responsible for critical revision of the manuscript. All authors 
approved the final version of the manuscript. JND is the guarantor.
Funding  The authors have not declared a specific grant for this research from any 
funding agency in the public, commercial or not-­for-­profit sectors.
Competing interests  None declared.
Patient consent for publication  Not applicable.
Ethics approval  This was a retrospective service evaluation of the use of 
anonymised patient data. The study was registered with the local clinical 
governance team. Formal ethical approval and individual patient consent were 
therefore not required. The study was conducted in compliance with principles 
outlined in the Declaration of Helsinki.
